title: SR-CGCNN: Shared Recurrent Convolution in Crystal Graph Neural Networks for Materials Property Prediction
abstract: Crystal graph neural networks predict materials properties by propagating information through local atomic environments. In conventional crystal graph convolutional neural networks (CGCNNs), this propagation depth is increased by stacking independently parameterized convolutional layers. This coupling between message-passing depth and parameter count raises a simple question: can repeated application of the same learned local update recover most of the benefit of a deeper CGCNN? We address this question by introducing a shared-recurrent CGCNN (SR-CGCNN), in which the main crystal-graph convolutional weights are tied across recurrent message-passing steps. The graph construction, pooling operation, and prediction head are kept unchanged, allowing a controlled comparison with standard CGCNN baselines. On Materials Project-derived formation-energy and band-gap datasets, a three-step SR-CGCNN approaches the accuracy of a standard three-layer CGCNN while using only $34.5\%$ of its trainable convolutional parameters. The formation-energy test mean absolute error changes from $0.0945$ to $0.0986~\mathrm{eV\,atom^{-1}}$, while the band-gap error changes from $0.4346$ to $0.4503~\mathrm{eV}$. These results indicate that repeated shared message passing can provide a parameter-efficient approximation to stacked CGCNN depth, offering a compact recurrent interpretation of crystal graph convolution.
summary: This paper proposes a more efficient version of Crystal Graph Neural Networks that uses shared weights across recurrent steps. This approach allows for deeper information propagation in crystal structures without a massive increase in the number of trainable parameters. It demonstrates that weight-tying can maintain high predictive accuracy for material properties like band gap and formation energy.
main_problem: The coupling between message-passing depth and parameter count in Crystal Graph Convolutional Neural Networks (CGCNNs) makes increasing the structural receptive field computationally expensive.
main_result: The proposed SR-CGCNN achieves accuracy comparable to standard CGCNNs while using only 34.5% of the trainable convolutional parameters by using weight-tying across recurrent steps.
method: The authors introduce a shared-recurrent architecture where the same convolutional weights are applied repeatedly across multiple message-passing steps to decouple depth from parameter count.
model_or_system: The model operates on crystalline materials represented as graphs, where atoms are nodes and interatomic distances define edges, using datasets from the Materials Project.
important_parameters: Number of message-passing steps (T) and number of independent convolutional layers (L).
main_assumptions: The primary benefit of deeper networks can be approximated by the repeated application of a single learned local update.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a schematic comparison between the architecture of standard stacked CGCNNs and the proposed recurrent SR-CGCNN.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the parameter-depth coupling problem, describes the SR-CGCNN architecture, presents benchmarking results on formation energy and band gap datasets, and discusses limitations and future directions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic comparison between the standard three- layer CGCNN and the proposed shared-recurrent CGCNN (SR-CGCNN). In the standard architecture, three message- passing steps are performed using independently parameter- ized convolutional blocks with parameters θ1, θ2, and θ3. In SR-CGCNN, the same message-passing transformation with shared weights θ is applied recurrently for the same number of propagation steps. The graph construction, pooling oper- ation, and prediction head are kept unchanged. In the imple- mentation used in this work, the main convolutional weights are shared across recurrent steps, while normalization param- eters are allowed to be step-specific.

FIG. 2. Test-set parity plots for the standard three-layer CGCNN and the proposed SR-CGCNN. (a) Formation- energy prediction. (b) Band-gap prediction. The dashed line denotes ideal agreement between reference and predicted values. The three-step SR-CGCNN closely follows the stan- dard CGCNN-3 prediction trend for both targets while re- ducing the number of convolutional parameters from 66,432 to 22,912.
